label: tetlock-est-q1
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: estimate ONE sub-question in isolation. You see only the facts and
this sub-question; you must not analyze the broader dispute. Give a calibrated
probability and the few considerations doing the real work.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

In the City of Rio Robles (invented), Councilmember Dana Okafor's spouse is the salaried Director of Programs (~$95,000/yr) at Harbor Light Alliance, a 501(c)(3) homeless-services provider. Under a 2019 city resolution, Okafor also sits on Harbor Light's board of directors, uncompensated, as the council's designated liaison.

The council is about to (a) adopt the framework and scoring criteria for a competitive RFP allocating $2.3M in combined city general-fund and state HHAP pass-through money for homelessness services, and then (b) award the resulting contract(s). Harbor Light is one of three realistic bidders and has historically won about 60% of the city's homelessness contracts. The spouse's position is funded out of Harbor Light's general operating budget, to which city contracts contribute roughly 40%.

Okafor asks the city attorney: May I vote on the RFP framework? On the award? Must I resign the board seat or must my spouse's employment change? And if I simply recuse, is the city safe contracting with Harbor Light?

## The question

Analyze under Government Code § 1090 (including whether board membership plus a spouse's salaried employment is a prohibited financial interest 'in' the contract, whether any § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest exception applies, and whether recusal even cures § 1090 for a board member), and under the Political Reform Act (§§ 87100, 87103 — community-property interest in spouse's income, materiality, and the public-generally exception), plus common-law conflict doctrine. What combination of resignation, recusal, wall procedures, or contract structuring lets the city lawfully fund Harbor Light, if any? What are the consequences of getting it wrong (§ 1090 voidness, disgorgement, criminal exposure, FPPC enforcement)?

## Sub-question to estimate (in isolation)

**Does Okafor have a disqualifying financial interest in a Harbor Light contract under Government Code section 1090, arising either from (a) the spouse's $95,000 salaried position (partially funded by city/HHAP money) or (b) Okafor's own uncompensated board seat, or both independently?**

What would have to be true: Research would need to confirm (i) whether case law/AG opinions treat a spouse's employment income as automatically creating a section 1090 interest for the official (independent of community-property PRA analysis), (ii) whether 'financial interest' under section 1090 requires potential personal pecuniary gain/loss and whether an uncompensated directorship satisfies that absent salary, and (iii) whether the city-contract share of Harbor Light's operating budget (40%) is sufficiently direct/foreseeable to make the spouse's job (and thus Okafor's interest) 'in' this specific RFP contract versus too attenuated.
text: {"probability":80,"key_considerations":["Section 1090's 'financially interested' standard is construed broadly and prophylactically (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633; Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565), and courts have imputed a spouse's financial stake to the official via community-property/quasi-community-property principles (Fam. Code § 760) even outside the PRA's explicit spousal-imputation text. People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289 appears closely on-point for spousal ties to a nonprofit receiving public funds and, if its facts/holding verify as recalled, supports finding a disqualifying interest independent of PRA § 87103 — this is the strongest single authority pushing the estimate upward, but its exact holding and procedural posture (statute-of-limitations reversal) need confirmation.","Government Code § 1091(b) contains an enumerated 'remote interest' category for noncompensated officers/directors of nonprofit corporations (verify exact subsection, plausibly (b)(8)), meaning the board seat alone is likely a curable remote interest via disclosure/abstention/noninvolvement rather than a fatal, non-curable interest by itself — this cuts against treating prong (b) as automatically voiding the contract, even though Okafor still could not vote on it.","The spouse's $95k salary is not obviously covered by any enumerated § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest category for spousal employment by a contracting nonprofit (no confirmed carve-out found), so if it counts as an 'interest' at all it likely falls outside the statutory cure mechanisms — making prong (a) the more legally serious basis and the one doing most of the work in a 'yes' conclusion, and potentially tainting reliance on the remote-interest cure for prong (b) as well under a totality-of-interests approach.","Attenuation is the real fight on prong (a): funds flow into Harbor Light's general operating budget (40% city-derived) rather than being earmarked to the spouse's salary line, and this specific $2.3M RFP is only part of that 40% stream. But the spouse's title — Director of Programs — suggests she likely administers the very programs this RFP would fund, which strengthens directness/foreseeability and cuts against an attenuation defense; AG opinions on how direct a funding nexus must be to trigger § 1090 need to be located and checked against this fact pattern.","Section 1090's threshold inquiry (is there a 'financial interest' at all) is analytically prior to and distinct from the § 1091/1091.5 exception analysis and from PRA materiality/public-generally analysis; this sub-question isolates only the threshold interest question, so the estimate should not be discounted for cure/remedy availability, only for whether an interest exists in the first place — which the breadth of 1090 case law and the Honig-type spousal-nonprofit fact pattern make more likely than not to exist for at least the spousal-salary prong."],"what_evidence_would_change_this":"Confirming the actual holding/facts of People v. Honig on the spousal-nonprofit-employment point would be decisive either way. Locating any AG opinion or case squarely addressing spousal salary from a nonprofit whose revenue is only partially (not directly/earmarked) derived from the specific contract at issue — and how courts draw the directness/foreseeability line — would materially move the estimate. Confirming the precise text of Government Code § 1091(b) and § 1091.5(a) to see whether any subsection covers spousal employment by a nonprofit contracting party (as opposed to just noncompensated director status) would also be decisive: if such a carve-out exists and applies, the probability should drop; if confirmed absent, it should rise further."}

key_considerations: Section 1090's 'financially interested' standard is construed broadly and prophylactically (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633; Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565), and courts have imputed a spouse's financial stake to the official via community-property/quasi-community-property principles (Fam. Code § 760) even outside the PRA's explicit spousal-imputation text. People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289 appears closely on-point for spousal ties to a nonprofit receiving public funds and, if its facts/holding verify as recalled, supports finding a disqualifying interest independent of PRA § 87103 — this is the strongest single authority pushing the estimate upward, but its exact holding and procedural posture (statute-of-limitations reversal) need confirmation.

Government Code § 1091(b) contains an enumerated 'remote interest' category for noncompensated officers/directors of nonprofit corporations (verify exact subsection, plausibly (b)(8)), meaning the board seat alone is likely a curable remote interest via disclosure/abstention/noninvolvement rather than a fatal, non-curable interest by itself — this cuts against treating prong (b) as automatically voiding the contract, even though Okafor still could not vote on it.

The spouse's $95k salary is not obviously covered by any enumerated § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest category for spousal employment by a contracting nonprofit (no confirmed carve-out found), so if it counts as an 'interest' at all it likely falls outside the statutory cure mechanisms — making prong (a) the more legally serious basis and the one doing most of the work in a 'yes' conclusion, and potentially tainting reliance on the remote-interest cure for prong (b) as well under a totality-of-interests approach.

Attenuation is the real fight on prong (a): funds flow into Harbor Light's general operating budget (40% city-derived) rather than being earmarked to the spouse's salary line, and this specific $2.3M RFP is only part of that 40% stream. But the spouse's title — Director of Programs — suggests she likely administers the very programs this RFP would fund, which strengthens directness/foreseeability and cuts against an attenuation defense; AG opinions on how direct a funding nexus must be to trigger § 1090 need to be located and checked against this fact pattern.

Section 1090's threshold inquiry (is there a 'financial interest' at all) is analytically prior to and distinct from the § 1091/1091.5 exception analysis and from PRA materiality/public-generally analysis; this sub-question isolates only the threshold interest question, so the estimate should not be discounted for cure/remedy availability, only for whether an interest exists in the first place — which the breadth of 1090 case law and the Honig-type spousal-nonprofit fact pattern make more likely than not to exist for at least the spousal-salary prong.
data.what_evidence_would_change_this: Confirming the actual holding/facts of People v. Honig on the spousal-nonprofit-employment point would be decisive either way. Locating any AG opinion or case squarely addressing spousal salary from a nonprofit whose revenue is only partially (not directly/earmarked) derived from the specific contract at issue — and how courts draw the directness/foreseeability line — would materially move the estimate. Confirming the precise text of Government Code § 1091(b) and § 1091.5(a) to see whether any subsection covers spousal employment by a nonprofit contracting party (as opposed to just noncompensated director status) would also be decisive: if such a carve-out exists and applies, the probability should drop; if confirmed absent, it should rise further.
